verses.text: The wider families of Judah — Pharez, Hur, Shobal, and the rest — are listed by their towns. The ordinary clans have their place. The chosen tribe was made up of countless households, each one known and numbered by God.

The descendants of Ashur, father of Tekoa, and others are recorded. These are names history would otherwise forget. But God remembers the obscure families of His people as carefully as the great.

Jabez, named for his mother's sorrow, becomes 'more honourable than his brethren' through prayer: that God would bless him, enlarge his border, and keep him from evil. And God granted it. No sorrowful beginning binds the soul that calls on God.

Jabez, named for his mother's sorrow, becomes 'more honourable than his brethren' through prayer: that God would bless him, enlarge his border, and keep him from evil. And God granted it. No sorrowful beginning binds the soul that calls on God.

More Judahite families are listed, including Othniel's line and craftsmen of the valley. Deliverers and workmen alike are named. God honours both those who fought His battles and those who plied honest trades.

The clans include Caleb son of Jephunneh, and even the daughter of Pharaoh married into Judah. A foreign princess is grafted in. God's covenant people were enriched by those who joined themselves from outside.

The list names potters and weavers who 'dwelt with the king for his work.' Humble tradesmen are honoured in the sacred record. Faithful labour, offered in service, is never beneath God's notice or His remembrance.

Simeon's families are recorded, noting that they 'had not many children' and did not multiply like Judah. The tribe rebuked by Jacob dwindled. The old word spoken over Simeon quietly worked itself out in history.

Simeon's towns within Judah's territory are listed, held 'unto the reign of David.' The smaller tribe had its place in the land. God gave each tribe its portion, even those that would later fade.

Simeon's villages and their genealogy close the section. The fading tribe is still carefully recorded. God does not despise the small or the diminishing, but numbers them among His own.
Simeon's villages and their genealogy close the section. The fading tribe is still carefully recorded. God does not despise the small or the diminishing, but numbers them among His own.
Simeon's princes are named, whose families 'increased greatly.' God gave growth to these households. Even within a fading tribe, the LORD blessed the faithful with increase and a future.

Some Simeonites push out to find pasture and finally destroy the remnant of Amalek. The ancient enemy is at last struck down. God's old judgments are fulfilled in His time, even centuries later, through His people who go forward in faith.
